# Delay estimation in practice

## What the data let you estimate

- In applied work the estimand is set by how the data were
  collected, not by which textbook delay you wanted
- A line list records exposure windows, onset dates, and a source
  attribution to the day, in real time
- So the serial interval is rarely estimated on its own: you fit the
  delays the data structure supports, and read it off
- Two recent real-data case studies make this concrete

::: {.callout-note}
Both are built on the same composed observation model as before:
primary and secondary censoring with real-time truncation, applied
record by record.
:::

## Andes virus: Epuyén, 2018-19

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
- First sustained person-to-person spread of a hantavirus: one
  zoonotic introduction, four human generations, super-spreading at
  social events
  ([Martínez et al. 2020](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2009040))
- The line list has two record types, so two estimands fall out:
  - *index* cases have a point exposure, giving the incubation period
  - *sourced* cases give source-onset to onset, the serial interval
- The serial interval is a convolution: signed transmission timing
  plus the secondary's incubation period, with the infection time
  unobserved
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.callout-tip}
## Data challenge
Exposure and onset to the day (interval censoring), real-time
cut-off (right truncation), and a transmission timing seen only
through the convolution, so it is weakly identified.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Lesson
The index cases pin the incubation period, which is what lets the
sourced cases identify transmission timing at all.
The serial interval is not estimated directly: it is what the two
delays imply.
:::
:::
::::

::: {.attribution}
Re-analysis and composed-stack fit:
[epiforecasts/andv-linelist-analysis](https://github.com/epiforecasts/andv-linelist-analysis)
·
[EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl)
· data from
[Martínez et al. 2020](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2009040)
:::

## Bundibugyo Ebola: Isiro, 2012

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
- The only public Bundibugyo line list, from the seven-outbreak DRC
  deposit
  ([Rosello et al. 2015](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.09015))
- One case carries four delays: onset to admission, admission to
  death or discharge, and onset to notification
- Resolution is competing risks: a case dies or is discharged, never
  both, and the split is the case-fatality ratio
- None of these is the serial interval; they are the delays the
  surveillance and care pathway actually recorded
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.callout-tip}
## Data challenge
Dates to the day, so every delay is doubly interval censored.
Several admission dates encode impossible offsets and a notification
delay was mis-coded upstream, both fixed before fitting.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Lesson
What you can estimate is dictated by the data: a care-pathway line
list yields onset-to-admission, bed-day, and surveillance-lag
delays, with the case-fatality ratio as a covariate-driven split.
The transmission interval is simply not in this data.
:::
:::
::::

::: {.attribution}
Bayesian re-analysis:
[epiforecasts/bdbv-linelist-analysis](https://github.com/epiforecasts/bdbv-linelist-analysis)
·
[EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl)
· data from
[Rosello et al. 2015](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.09015)
:::
